description: Dialect of the Kabenyong language spoken in the port city of Sabohai.

entries.definition: just now, in the recent past

examples.original: tin o acéncia ki anyai pengkom

entries.definition: be fair to or with s.o.

examples.original: dé’é Hanggara odam adéh ki aban
examples.translation: The Hanggara are not fair to us.

entries.definition: blessed, personal name (var. **aditena**). This shortening is considered improper, so *aditena* is usually used instead.

examples.original: wagésén odam encia aditena-n holih ki dé
examples.translation: The priest didn’t want to bless my child.

derivations.definition: low-ranking priest tasked with blessing new children, weddings, etc.

examples.original: gibau to’ oka’ adung ki pen
examples.translation: This food is the one for you.
The river there is very narrow.

derivations.definition: start to close up, become thin
be frustrated due to mental overload or confusion

examples.translation: I am at wit’s end (More literally “my thoughts have thinned”)
Show me both of your hands.

examples.original: dé o do-nyéni agia ki dé
examples.translation: I yelled at my younger sibling.
There are not a lot of snakes here.

examples.original: umau to’ o agoh ki regai
examples.translation: This fruit doesn’t have much flesh.
Remove some water from the broth!

examples.original: cé’ jo-no agum udoh ta’ kua apo’!
examples.translation: Don’t expect there to be someone to clean up after you!
He should go, he is allowed to go.

examples.original: dé o hén do-pu’ kat-ahi’, geping hén silun ki dé o nyosé’
examples.translation: I just tripped and fell, so now my foot hurts.
He’s the one who knocked over the vessel.

derivations.definition: push something off, cause to fall with great intent

examples.translation: Jumping into the river is fun.

examples.original: tin o ambana ki bah ahun
cé’ bah-o darong, tin o ajeng
pen o lu’ jo-mau hanyi’, ahét jen-au ajeng
examples.translation: If you’re feeling sick, you should lie down.

examples.original: dé o ompera ki pen nin ajeng
examples.translation: I know you’re pretending to be asleep.
I will put you to sleep.

derivations.definition: to take water with a bucket

entries.definition: uncertain, indecisive, aimless, person with unclear goals

examples.original: tin o neméng ra-silun ki akaseka’
examples.translation: He has a habit of walking around aimlessly.

examples.original: bedai akaseka’ o cé’ kua teka’

derivations.definition: say odd things that don’t make sense

examples.original: ho~hobang o udoh tisom bah akaseka’
examples.translation: Elderly people sometimes say strange things.

examples.original: Adina o ambana ki ben akaseka’

derivations.definition: daydream, let thoughts wander, lost in thought

examples.original: damo, dé o do-pu’ jo akaseka’
examples.translation: Sorry, I was lost in my thoughts.

examples.original: lu’ aken-aken-au soh akaseka’, peji ki pen o kau karong
examples.translation: If you keep fidgeting, your fingers will fall off.

examples.original: dé o hén jo aken ne’-ne’

derivations.definition: to repeat many times, keep on doing something (of a habit), more and more (of an inchoative verb).

examples.original: dé o doh-oken-aken meléh pa inang
examples.translation: I have gone there many times.
The rain is getting heavier and heavier.

derivations.definition: say something over and over again

examples.original: dé o do-boh aken-aken pa tin

derivations.definition: to be continually reminded of something

examples.original: basa ki dé, tin o nin aken-aken
examples.translation: In my opinion, he is a pathological liar.

examples.original: cemia mai o hemai sina’ akia
examples.translation: The river has a lot of fish.
He is courageous (having a lot of blood)

entries.definition: time in far past; a long time ago

examples.translation: Long ago, this village was a forest.
The beginning of the *gecarungon* festival (a harvest festival) is loud.

examples.original: sina’ ta’ aliting o udoh agoh

entries.definition: ancestral, passed down from generation to generation

examples.translation: The village chief’s house is passed down from generation to generation.

examples.original: pen o lu’ jemé’-au, bong sina’ ancéla si pen?
examples.translation: When you go fishing, what fish do you want?

entries.definition: to suffer, suffocate in something, perish

derivations.definition: to die a violent death (lit. “drown in blood”)

examples.original: anga arah oka’ dé cé’ encia
examples.translation: I don’t want to die violently.

examples.original: bibang ki dé o doh-onga hobang
examples.translation: My grandma died of old age.

derivations.definition: to die by being buried alive

examples.original: Kakadom o doh-onga minyau réng lenga’ si péa ta’ tin do-kiro’
examples.translation: Kakadom (mythological figure) was buried alive by the rubble of the house he destroyed.

derivations.definition: The mountain is obstructed by that house.

examples.translation: This tree is blocking the sun.

examples.original: tin o do-é kat-angara-n pa lekit
tin o angét ki udoh pa péa

entries.definition: to enable, allow, grant an ability

examples.original: cogua sinjau to’ o angkina ki ahi’-en umau mai
examples.translation: This long pole lets me knock the fruit off.
Eels are common in the swamp.

entries.definition: to have (of a person’s material possessions)

examples.translation: You are smelly, go take a bath!
It smells like a corpse here.

examples.original: Wirap o ahét anyung-en-au holih ki tin
hon o lu’ cé’ kat-anyung-en-au, geping ru’ ahét arai gelon
examples.translation: If one does not behave oneself, one will run into trouble.

examples.original: pa lu’ soan-au bedai Hanggara, hon o ahét bah-au anyung
examples.translation: When meeting a Hanggara, one should talk politely.

examples.original: tin o jo ao’a si hekau ki tin

entries.definition: a greeting (not used as an exclamation, see **jongon**)
to continue, keep on doing something, without pause
be absent, missing, not present, unavailable

examples.translation: There is no food on me.

derivations.definition: to have something missing, be free of something

examples.original: ridih mai o apoh ki igau
examples.translation: That lizard is missing a tail.
We need to get rid of the water.

derivations.definition: to pull out and remove (small hairs, weeds, etc.)

examples.original: he~henda-bai oka’ pen udu’ soh-au apoh-en dingai
examples.translation: You need to remove the hairs first.

derivations.definition: to run away, (of a person) disappear

entries.definition: be an only child (of a person)

examples.translation: Oh, she only has one child?
Has the trash been thrown away?

derivations.definition: badly made or of low quality such that it is thrown away. An insult.*po’ udoh bedai ta’ ambana ki helau **p~apo’-ai** ki pen?*

examples.original: cé’ jo-no agum udoh ta’ kua apo’!
examples.translation: Don’t expect there to be someone to clean up after you!

derivations.definition: toss away something, to spit on the ground

examples.original: odon o tu apo’ nyé’ pa cemia
examples.translation: Just throw the bones into the river.

derivations.definition: to bleed, to leak, have a leak (syn. **lérah** ‘to bleed’)

examples.original: jombang nyé’a to’ o ra’-arah amo
examples.translation: This damn boat has a leak again.

entries.definition: to pass by, meet by chance
open (of containers, or doors and pathways)

derivations.definition: to open (a container, or doors and pathways)

examples.original: danja’ dé o ru’ ararai ki gelumau caru
examples.translation: Tomorrow I will be busy with harvesting *caru*.

derivations.definition: to busily go here and there, back and forth

examples.original: tin o do-é ararai pa péa pa megau
examples.translation: He was going back and forth between his house and farm.

entries.definition: a freshwater crab found in rivers

examples.original: ra’on-ra’on si Hanggara o nyéh asa’-en aban
examples.translation: All the Hanggara do is oppress us.

derivations.definition: A fire spirit said to be found in swamps

examples.translation: He takes a long time to eat.
She carried the harvest on her shoulders.

examples.original: cada-ngon si dé o nyé’ ason

entries.definition: money, the currency used by the Hanggara (< Hanggara *atak*)

examples.original: cé’ titi’-o atek, mai o puncau
examples.translation: Don’t touch the sap, it’s sticky.

examples.original: dé o doh-otia ki rangau tin
examples.translation: I hit him on his back.

derivations.definition: hit from far away (by throwing, shooting, etc.)

examples.original: céi tu-mau atia ngélé’ mai go ulu
examples.translation: Try hitting that pig with rocks.

examples.original: Adina o ambana ki bah atia bedai ki ahun
examples.translation: Adina likes to insult people in funny ways.

derivations.definition: a wooden slit drum played with a mallet.

examples.original: pen o yo’ ra-sebia ki até’a?
pa lu’ séla’-au ne’-ne’, cé’ pu’-o ra-hon yugua atora
examples.translation: When burning something, don’t breathe in the resulting smoke.

examples.original: jugua mai o nyéh atora ki bedai gentih
examples.translation: That smoke is just from someone roasting food.

examples.original: kirung ki dé o doh-otua niging
examples.translation: My elbow bumped into the wall
I accidentally brushed against a nettle.

examples.original: dé o do-boh atua gegesah ta’ mai

derivations.definition: topic that is talked about, things said
to talk, speak (used in poetry for an extra syllable)

examples.translation: Don’t say “I don’t want to” (Part of a song where each line is eight syllables).

derivations.definition: to have a casual conversation, chit-chat

examples.original: dé o do-bun bagah nga’ hadu
examples.translation: I had a chat with my friends.

examples.original: Wirap o ambana ki bagah-bagah si holih ki dé
examples.translation: Wirap likes to go on and on about her children.

examples.original: pen o yo’a ko’-au bagah pa tin si mai po’?
examples.translation: Could you talk to her (for me) about it?

derivations.definition: to lie, trick someone with words

examples.original: tin o do-nin bagah dé in cada
empua ki dé o do-nyo’ bagah in adé
examples.translation: My mother gave a stern talking to me.

entries.definition: therefore, thus (var. **geping**). Usually only found in poetry for an extra syllable.
because (var. **goming**). Usually only found in poetry for an extra syllable.
MV of verbs relating to speech or producing sound, can also be followed by an utterance. This word is also used with onomatopoeias to mean “produce such sound”

examples.translation: The boat made a creaking sound.

derivations.definition: any illness that “affects the soul” and requires rituals to cure

examples.original: bedai ta’ puken-au ka-balai o udu’ henung-au benyong si yahakai
examples.translation: People that get a magic illness must drink water from a spring.

examples.original: holih ki dé o do-ro-balai ki pindi-pindi

derivations.definition: give someone an illness by magic

examples.original: aban o ru’ ulih-au dé’é go to-balai in réngom
examples.translation: We shall defeat them by giving their warriors illnesses with magic.

derivations.definition: contagious, (of a negative thing) spreads quickly

examples.original: bamo ki tin o laba henda

entries.definition: type of bird with long beak, heron or egret

examples.translation: The fabric is soaked in water.

examples.original: ka~karun si dé o do-ko-baoh éba do-suman
examples.translation: My clothes became wet because it rained.

entries.definition: according to, as claimed by someone

examples.original: basa ki dé, pen o ahét ko’-au bagah tin
examples.translation: In my opinion, you should tell her.

examples.original: mai o cé’ mékin basa ki tin
holih ki alo o basau endi
examples.translation: You two’s child will probably be ugly.

examples.original: mai o ohomau ki nikim pa batim
examples.translation: It’s better done during the day.

examples.original: péa si tin o bebeh ki cemia
examples.translation: His house is across the river.

examples.original: kohau topong-o dé in bebeh-en lindin to’
examples.translation: Please help me bring this stuff across.

derivations.definition: to have intercourse to have a baby

examples.original: dé odam ambana ki beho regai
examples.translation: I don’t like to eat meat.

derivations.definition: grab something to eat, eat with hands

examples.translation: Go ahead, grab a fruit to eat.

derivations.definition: to make waves in the water

examples.original: silun ki tin o do-to-beléh pa benyong
examples.translation: His foot made waves in the water.

entries.definition: have a difference, characteristic (possibly related to **belia** ‘different’ ?)

examples.original: canginti o beléna ki laba rurugua
examples.translation: A characteristic of the *Canginti* (a type of tree) is that it has thorns.

examples.original: kengkah ki tubé cemia o belia-belia
umau helai to’ o belia ki reta
examples.translation: These two fruits are different in color.

derivations.definition: to change, influence something (syn. **habai** ‘to replace, switch’)

examples.original: dé o do-bulé’-en wendéme’ si tin

derivations.definition: to separate (intransitive), e.g. of a mixture

examples.original: imbong pen o nyé’ do-dunggun benyong, obo’ odam nyé’ do-é   ka-belia!
examples.translation: Why have you already added the water, the oil has not yet separated!

entries.definition: MV of leisurely or playful actions. Can be combined with the name of a game to form a verb meaning to play said game.

examples.original: tubé ge’ai o bengga ki cemia
examples.translation: Every village is connected by the river.

examples.original: aban o do-boh bengga si tin
aban o do-kua bengga bitau péa
examples.translation: We worked together to build a house.
Gather all the heads of houses of the village.

examples.original: lumpo benyong o ka-bengga pa injah
examples.translation: All the water comes together at the river mouth.

derivations.definition: to come together (of people or animals)

examples.original: aban o ru’ ka-bengga-n-au pa ka-péa
examples.translation: We will gather in the hall.

derivations.definition: to hang out, come together for fun

examples.original: érung o ahét ben ka-bengga-n-au kau-kau
examples.translation: We two should hang out sometime.

derivations.definition: a term referring to four major sacred rivers on the Sesenjuh peninsula, which represents the four times the mother goddess has given birth, also an exonym for the people living around the four rivers used by the Hanggara. In most contexts it is applied to a specific ethnolinguistic group which the Hanggara have the most contact with.

examples.original: béhia si sina’ o udoh akia
examples.translation: There are many types of fish.

examples.original: ahét aban o bah-au béhia in Inca’ Kampoh
examples.translation: Let us chant for Kampoh (fertility goddess and one of the creators of the world).

examples.original: po’ pen o encia jen béhia nga’ dé?
examples.translation: Do you want to dance with me?

entries.definition: a personal name and the name of a folk hero
the outside or outermost part of something

examples.original: bija ki péa si dé oka’ dé encia ra-reta
examples.translation: I want to color the outside of my house.

derivations.definition: to talk about something in little detail

examples.original: tin o nyéh do-boh bija si pen
examples.translation: He only talked a little bit about you.

derivations.definition: to walk around, along the edge something

examples.original: dé o ambana ki wé bija pa bomi
examples.translation: I like to walk along the beach.
I have never built a house.

derivations.definition: to conduct a major construction effort, overhaul

examples.original: tajombang oka’ salan encia nyo’ bitau
examples.translation: The chief wants to develop the port.
Onoru’s belly is kind of big.
Ritual conflict is not like a war.
I got into a fight with those two.
You’re the one that started the fight!

examples.original: dé o agum Hanggara cé’ to-ka-boih-au nga’ aban
examples.translation: I hope the Hanggara don’t start a war with us.

examples.original: dé o ambana ki wé bija pa bomi
examples.translation: I like to walk along the beach.
Where’s a place with lots of clams?

examples.original: silun ki dé o do-pu’ buhot ulu
examples.translation: My foot got caught on a rock.

entries.definition: A type of epiphytic plant with long leaves in the family Araceae
entries.notes: Kabenyong explain: *Nenta to’ o angét ki udoh pa lekit pa ro’o ki* *cemia. Ratohet si tin o nganiging ki oé’. Repah ki tin ta’ sinjau, mai oka’ aban onggit in lap taho’ pipirobai*. ‘This plant usually grows in forests around rivers. It grows sticking on trees. We use its long leaves to wrap *pipirobai* (a cone-shaped dish).’

entries.definition: beside or nearby, but not touching

examples.original: tin o sudua pa cabi’ ki oé’
examples.translation: He is sitting beside the tree.

examples.original: senggia aro’ o udoh cacau piruh

examples.translation: The wind is blowing strong now.

derivations.definition: to make wind, e.g. by flapping a fan
to blow something with the mouth

entries.definition: to work (conduct some productive physical activity)

examples.translation: I work in the sea (a fisherman).

examples.original: dé odam yo’ jirai, udoh ca~cada
examples.translation: I can’t join, I have work.

examples.original: tin o ben cada celoren gelu’
examples.translation: He carves wood as a hobby

derivations.definition: to help someone with their work (syn. **topong** ‘to help’, **kua nikim** ‘to help’)

entries.definition: a type of grain; staple crop

examples.original: caru oka’ dé ru’ gelumu’-au dingai

derivations.definition: A malevolent spirit associated with excess grain.

examples.original: oé’ mai o kau ahét cele’

derivations.definition: to tip something over, such as by pushing or pulling, fell a tree.

examples.original: cogua mai oka’ aban ru’ cele’-en-au go rotau
examples.translation: We will topple the pole by pulling it (with a rope).

derivations.definition: to be hit by an object that has tipped over
to hit the ground after being tipped over

examples.original: étan oé’ mai odam to’-au cele’ pa péa si dé
examples.translation: I hope that tree doesn’t fall on my house.

entries.definition: to mold, form, carve into a shape

derivations.definition: to mold with hands (as opposed to using a tool)

examples.original: télé oka’ tin do-soh celoren ki pucan
examples.translation: He molded the clay into a cup.

derivations.definition: to shape by pounding, to smith (informal)

examples.original: ida oka’ aban enggo’-en in celua
examples.translation: We heat the metal so it is malleable.

examples.original: holih o jo celua lu’ réng dahom ki dé’é
examples.translation: Children are easily influenced by their parents.

examples.original: dé o ru’ ra-cemé’-au pa Taselohon
examples.translation: I will go by river to Taselohon.

entries.definition: a personal name, probably cognate with Tembendié *cendue* “amazing” and Hanggara *centur* “true”

examples.original: péa si dé o centa’ senyah

derivations.definition: to sit or stand idly and watch surroundings

examples.original: kinoning dé o hén ben-au centa’ nyé’
examples.translation: Looks like I’ll just idle now.

examples.original: aban o aro’ jen centa’ ngasonyoh

derivations.definition: to think about or have a goal in mind

examples.original: tin o jo centa’ anyai pengkom
examples.translation: His goal is to be rich.

examples.original: yahakai o mula udoh-au pa cepun

derivations.definition: to think about, figure out the reason or cause of something

examples.original: jo-no cepun yabagom ki pen o ligu

derivations.definition: to make an effort to do something

examples.original: tin o nyo’ céi penjon paloh
examples.translation: He’s trying to learn how to swim.

entries.definition: negation or prohibitive particle for verbs, adjectives, and adverbs

derivations.definition: to suddenly shout something at someone

examples.original: tin o do-boh cobé pa dé

derivations.definition: to throw (used in poetry for an extra syllable)
to throw hard, with great intent (e.g. a spear)

examples.translation: Of course a house has poles.

entries.definition: shortening of **cuah** used in the names of roots, rhizomes, and the like

derivations.definition: a type of tuber not unlike a cassava
spiraling tendrils of any plant, used by children for entertainment, but also as an aphrodisiac
the roots of a bush, used to cure constipation and stomachaches
the thick aerial roots of an unidentified plant used to tie wrapped foods, it is said to impart a particular bitter aroma to foods
the thick roots of a plant that people often chew on
the roots of a plant used in shamanistic rituals, likely contains a hallucinogen

examples.original: imbong cupéh ki pen o ga’ mara?
examples.translation: Why is your skin so white?

examples.original: pen o encia to’ da mai?
examples.translation: Do you want this or that?

examples.original: dahom o udu’ daha-mau holih ki tin
examples.translation: A parent must take care of their child.

entries.definition: parent or parents (possibly a shortening of *daha-hom*)

examples.translation: You two have the same height.

derivations.definition: be the same in some aspect

examples.translation: We two have the same height.

examples.original: dé o encia da~daih si to’
dé o jo daih nga’ tin

examples.translation: Just make their colors the same.

derivations.definition: to copy a pose or posture

examples.original: kula’ ki gibau si dé oka’ dé dalah-au
examples.translation: I will give you some of my food.

derivations.definition: to give out of kindness, for charity

examples.original: tin o do-kua dalah akia caru si tin
examples.translation: He gave up a lot of his *caru*.

derivations.definition: to bribe, gift in return for an immoral favor
to forcefully give something, insist that it be accepted
to shout, talk loudly, make a loud noise
to make a loud noise, shout a noise

examples.original: pa lu’ dé o ka-darong-en-au, wé meni’-o
dé o mula apa-mau dekai pen

derivations.definition: to make a thin layer of something

examples.translation: He made the wood into a thin layer.

entries.definition: tribe, nation. There are fourteen *dempu* that control villages around the Ungom, Amenai, Baonom, and Tatango rivers.

examples.translation: He is not inside the house.
Put the fruit into the basket.

derivations.definition: to hide into a hole or burrow

examples.translation: The *kurukap* (lizard sp.) hid in a hole.
The mountains are very far away.

derivations.definition: places other than the Sesenjuh peninsula
to move something away, shoo an animal

examples.translation: The boat is moving farther away.

derivations.definition: (with a noun) tires a specific part of the body, (with a verb) difficult to be accomplished

examples.translation: Don’t make it difficult for others.

examples.original: hajo’ suman, ka~karun si dé o embo derong
examples.translation: Even though it is raining, my clothes are still dry.

derivations.definition: to dry oneself, e.g. after bathing

examples.translation: Dry yourself before entering the house!

entries.definition: to resemble (visually, auditorily, or in other sensible properties such as taste, texture, and smell. Compare **tebe’** ‘be comparable to’)

examples.original: ulu mai o désa ki helau
examples.translation: That rock looks like a face

entries.definition: 3rd person plural pronoun, plural marker of human nouns

examples.original: to’ o dideng rona dahom ki dé do-ko-jirua
examples.translation: This is the place both my parents died.

examples.original: dé o do-boh diku hadu ki dé

derivations.definition: to get someone to come by throwing something at them

examples.translation: Don’t call me by throwing something at me.

derivations.definition: to have a feeling, feel an emotion, have a mental feeling, hunch

examples.original: dé o jo dinya tin hanyi’
examples.translation: I feel that she is sick.

derivations.definition: to empathize with (syn. **kua lentai** ‘consider, be aware of other people’, **kua opia** ‘consider what others may think’)

examples.original: tebe’ tin odam yo’ kua dinya yo’in
examples.translation: It’s as if he can’t empathize with others.

examples.original: dé o encia lap dinya umau
examples.translation: I want to taste the fruit.

examples.original: yo’an-o dé soh dinya tinyu ki pen

entries.definition: to pray, give an offering, conduct religious ceremony

examples.original: tin o do-boh disi pa bomi
soh-au disi in Inca’ Doh Ungom
dé o ru’ wé disi pa yahakai
examples.translation: I will travel to a spring.

examples.original: étan Inca’ Doh Ungom o diten-au megau to’
examples.translation: May Doh Ungom (goddess of the Ungom river) enrich this farm.

examples.original: toron-toron si tin o di’en dé
Dagua odam nyé’ bah di’en bedai

derivations.definition: to tell a comforting lie, white lie, or dishonest compliment

examples.original: dé o jo dinya bagah si tin nyéh nin di’en
examples.translation: I feel that what she has said are just false compliments.

examples.original: dé o do-soh di’en in tin
examples.translation: I have given many gifts to her.

entries.definition: patron deity of a river or body of fresh water, always female

examples.original: dé o do-sulua dohua ki ngélé’
examples.translation: I cut open the pig’s stomach.

entries.definition: to cause an event, induce a feeling, thought, or sickness (syn. **jelu’** ‘to cause an event’)

examples.original: tin do-ro’-incai pa cemia mai o do-dora’ sina’ ka-jirua
examples.translation: Him urinating in the river caused the fish to die.

examples.original: pen o do~dora’ ligu in dé
gibau mai o kau dora’ tébia
examples.translation: That food will give you diarrhea.

examples.original: bedai pa duhau ki Adina o Karah
examples.translation: The person on the right of Adina is Karah.
The right side of the house is dirty.

entries.definition: memories, experiences, the past of someone’s life

examples.original: dé odam encia gela’ si dungua ki dé
examples.translation: I don’t want to think about my past.

derivations.definition: to recount memories, to vent about past life experiences

examples.original: dé o udu’ bedai in kua rengo bah dungua si dé
examples.translation: I need someone to listen to my venting.

examples.original: udoh tisom pa dé o jo dungua
examples.translation: There are times when I reminisce on the past.
Both his eyes are cloudy (has cataracts).

examples.original: aban o embo dengka réng ge’ai
examples.translation: We are still far from the village.

examples.original: dé o hemai empere’ in pen
examples.translation: I have some news for you.
How are you? (lit. what is your news?)

derivations.definition: deliver a spoken message or news
It would be better for you to deliver the message.

examples.original: mai oka’ pen udu’ bah-au empau

derivations.definition: to tell a lie, give false information

examples.original: empua ki dé o aro’ ra-megau
examples.translation: My mother is working on the field.

derivations.definition: give birth for the first time (syn. **tu to-bedai** ‘give birth’, **entu** ‘give birth’)

examples.translation: She does not want to have a child.

derivations.definition: be a mother to a child, euphemism for breastfeeding
to verbally ask for something, make a wish

examples.original: dé o nyé’ do-boh encia mai
examples.translation: I have already asked for it.

derivations.definition: be tired in a specific body part

examples.translation: I am getting more and more tired.
Let’s go before it gets foggy.
The meat is heated by the fire.

examples.original: pa enjau ki megau udoh oé’
examples.translation: There is a tree in the middle of the farm.

derivations.lexeme: *ka-ntai** ki oé’ to’ o ga’ tanga
derivations.definition: This tree’s trunk is very big.

entries.definition: to originate from (as a property of the subject)

derivations.definition: come from a progenitor (e.g. mother, or originator of an idea)

examples.original: bitau péa to’ o entung ki intang kandia
examples.translation: Building this house was the chief’s idea.

examples.original: dé o entung réng ge’ai Sabohai

derivations.definition: come from a group (e.g. lineage, tribe)

examples.original: dé o entung si hekau Tisup
examples.translation: I am of the Tisup family.

examples.original: dé o do-buho éba do-jo repai
examples.translation: I ate because I was hungry

examples.original: dé o ambana ki wé rasilun éjih
examples.translation: I like to go walking alone.

entries.definition: to be worried that something may happen

examples.original: dé o émba ki tin ka-jiro’-au
examples.translation: I am worried that she may die.

examples.original: dé o udu’ cada-mau, én dé o hanyi’
examples.translation: I have to work, but I am sick.

examples.original: dé o jo épuen go tin
dé o épuna ki tin ya’ mangkup
examples.translation: I am not sure he will win.

entries.definition: a type of tree with large leaves. The wood can be used for incense and the sap can be used as a poison for hunting small mammals.

examples.original: aro’ o étua, ro aban o koli’-au panjatun
examples.translation: It’s low tide now, let’s go look for crabs.

entries.definition: village, shortening of **ge’ai** used in toponyms

examples.translation: I am just waiting for good things.

entries.definition: a type of fish with lots of fats and oil, found around deltas.

examples.original: pa lu’ jemé’-au, dé o mula agum buhod-au gaharo
examples.translation: When fishing, I always hope to catch a *gaharo*.

entries.notes: Kabenyong explain: *nikim gecarungon o adung ki Inca’ Kampoh, Inca’ Doh ki cemia pon salahua sabéhia ta’ doditen, dosukehen megau* *si aban. Dé’é o nyé’ dohongkina ki ramegau pon beho, geping aban o udu’ ko’au pacuh di’enon in dé’é.* ‘Celebrating gecarungon is done for Kampoh, the *Doh* (riparian patron deity) of the river, and the various spirits that have enriched and fertilized our farms. They have allowed us to farm and eat, so we must return our thanks to them.’

derivations.definition: think about someone (in a positive way)

examples.original: dé o mula ko’-au gela’ tin

examples.translation: All he does is make problems.

examples.original: gelu juli o yo’ pa tisom-tisom
dé o do-kua nikim wé hemai ge~gelu in tin
examples.translation: I helped her carry the harvest.

derivations.definition: type of wood prized for its strength

examples.original: sebia ki sengka mai o genai
jen-o genai nyé’, cé’ udu’ jen kiat
examples.translation: Just relax, no need to be stiff.

derivations.definition: walk leisurely, take a stroll (syn. **ben ra-silun** ‘walk around’)

examples.translation: I’m going to walk around now.

derivations.definition: to pat or rub someone gently to calm them down

examples.translation: Let the boar calm down first.

derivations.definition: to learn to enjoy something, get used to

examples.translation: I have learned to enjoy cooking.

examples.original: ge~genjé ki ge’ia ki dé o nyosé’
examples.translation: The entirety of my body is in pain.

derivations.definition: get too hot from being near a fire

examples.original: cé’ jeoh pa sapa, kua pu’ gentih
examples.translation: Don’t be too close to the fire, you’ll get too hot.

examples.original: dé o repai, geping dé o encia beho
examples.translation: I am hungry, therefore I want to eat.
I will make food for them.

examples.original: dé o do-gusah tin udoh holih
examples.translation: I heard that he has a child.

derivations.definition: to spread a rumor or gossip

examples.original: dé o do-pu’ gesah dé’é do-bogah si dé
examples.translation: I overheard them talking about me.

examples.original: getin o ga’ piti hibi to’
examples.translation: The moon is so bright tonight.

examples.original: ge’ai o udoh akia pa bomi
examples.translation: There are many villages on the coast.

derivations.definition: city (typically applied to major port settlements)

examples.original: otia si nenta to’ o géhét

examples.translation: Shape the dough into a disc.

derivations.definition: curl up (of a millipede, isopod, or pangolin)
There is a pile of stuff.

examples.translation: The leaf litter is piling up.

examples.original: oé’ mai o gian ki lengo’

entries.definition: food (a general term for both humans and animals)

examples.original: dé o ambana ki gibau hapota

examples.translation: And then the rocks tumbled down.
The heavy rain caused a landslide.

derivations.definition: get swept or hit by tumbling objects

entries.definition: a single-edged sword resembling a large *matoka*.

derivations.definition: a sword that slightly curves back.

entries.definition: instrumental preposition, cause of some state

examples.original: mai oka’ dé ru’ teni’-en-au go kulém
examples.translation: I will fasten it later with a rope.

derivations.definition: The mountain is obstructed by that house.

examples.original: tin odam yo’ jirai, goming tin o hanyi’
examples.translation: He could not attend for he is sick.

examples.original: goning o ga’ akia pai pa repah mai
examples.translation: There is a lot of dew on that leaf.

entries.definition: ornative, preposition marking something worn, carried, or attached

examples.original: oé’ ha repah tangau mai oka’ aban tor-imia “ési”
examples.translation: We call that tree with big leaves “ési.”

entries.definition: to replace, switch something (syn. **belé’-en** ‘to change something’)

examples.original: dé o encia ra-hadu ki tin
tin o embo do-jirai hajo’ nyosé’ ki bohua
examples.translation: He still joined even though his belly ached.

examples.original: ta’ to’ o haloh ki reta
examples.translation: This one has a good color.
He wants to improve his swimming.

derivations.definition: to improve, heal (syn. **ka-juo** ‘to become healthy’, **libalai** ‘to heal’)
to improve in a specific part

examples.translation: I have gotten better at cooking.

examples.original: dé’é réngom o ka-haloh-en ki riang ki samasa’
examples.translation: The soldiers are practicing their sword-fighting.

derivations.definition: to reflect on oneself for self-improvement

examples.translation: You should go reflect on yourself.

derivations.definition: be sick due to a disease, aching in a body part

examples.original: dé o ambana ki gibau hapota

entries.definition: liver, believed to contain the soul
nuclear family: mother, father, and children

examples.original: hekau ki dé o nyéh laba bedai tapa’
examples.translation: My family only has four people.

derivations.definition: extended family that lives in a family compound, including servants if any.
marry and have children, have a family

examples.translation: Does he already have a family?

examples.original: dé o anyai nyéh bibin helai

derivations.definition: the second one, personal name (usually of second-born children)
to snap into two with hands.

examples.original: matoka si dé o do-to’ ka-helai, aro’ nyé’ nyo’ah-ai
examples.translation: My machete snapped in two, it’s useless now.

examples.original: méne’ to’ o helang ki benyong
examples.translation: This dough has too much water.

examples.original: bagah si pen o helang ki darong

examples.translation: You look happy (lit. happy in the face)

examples.original: dé o emba pa lu’ tin to-helu’-au
examples.translation: I am happy when she smiles.

examples.original: bong ta’ pen o soh hemai?
umau oka’ aban wé hemai go mecimih

derivations.definition: crowded with something, many individuals found together

examples.original: lekit to’ o hemé ki oé’
examples.translation: The forest is dense with trees.
Mix together the sap and oil.

derivations.definition: to gather together at a festival or game

examples.translation: Let’s go together to watch the fight.

derivations.definition: to mix in cooking, cook with something

examples.original: regai o angét ki aban lap hemé-n ocong, paran
examples.translation: We often cook meat with *ocong* and salt.

derivations.definition: to carry something with multiple people

examples.original: ulu to’ oka’ aban udu’ soh-au hemé
examples.translation: We need multiple people to carry this rock.

examples.original: henda ki dé o nyé’ do-hunda ka-mara
examples.translation: My hair has begun to whiten.

derivations.definition: hairs or fibers of a plant

examples.original: he~henda-bai oka’ pen udu’ soh-au apoh-en dingai
examples.translation: You need to remove the hairs first.

derivations.definition: a ritual where water is drunk from a spring

examples.original: tin o jo hepa in hényé hulat
examples.translation: He is committed to finding an eel.

derivations.definition: to go straight, continue onwards, (idiomatically) keep going despite adversities

entries.definition: to be sure, to believe, to trust

examples.original: aban o ru’ meléh pa hibi
ge’ai mai o hogia ki kabedai
examples.translation: That village is tucked away in the mountains.

entries.definition: self (reflexive or emphatic), generic pronoun, reflexive or reciprocal marker (accompanied by reduplication of the head verb)

examples.original: dé o yo’ geru’-au hon si dé
examples.translation: I can see my own self (myself).

examples.original: po’ hon si pen odam jo kenja in dé?
examples.translation: Do you yourself not care about me?

examples.original: hon o ahét kua riang in ka-haloh-en
po’ pen o yo’ geru’-au hon?

examples.translation: They two look at each other.

derivations.definition: to involuntarily smell, breathe in an odor

entries.definition: soul, a bodily fluid that carries “life force.” It is said that parents sacrifice some of this vital fluid when conceiving a child, and not wasting it is key to health, longevity, and possibly immortality (along with other “good” fluids).
a type of freshwater eel, colloquial term for eels in general

examples.translation: Catching eels by hand is hard.

entries.definition: year, defined as the time between two *gecarungon* festivals

examples.original: dé o ambana ki rengo hungon
dé’é odam do-ro-hungon si h~ahun si dé
examples.translation: They did not laugh at my joke.

examples.original: Adina o mula céi-mau to-hungon bedai
examples.translation: Adina always tries to make people laugh.

entries.definition: to overflow (of a container or river)

examples.translation: The river overflowed because it rained.

examples.original: hekau mai o ga’ hemé, péa ki dé’é o iduh ki bedai
examples.translation: That family is so big, their house is overflowing with people.

derivations.definition: to achieve a sort of enlightenment or gain a supernatural power

examples.original: tubé bedai o laba hasai ijih

derivations.definition: the first one, a personal name (usually for firstborn children)
the only one, the one and only

examples.original: ka~karun si pen o udoh ima

derivations.definition: to poke a hole in something

examples.original: pen o do-tor-ima pa ka~karun si dé
examples.translation: You poked a hole in my shirt.

examples.original: imbong cupéh ki pen o ga’ mara?
examples.translation: Why is your skin so white?

examples.original: imbong pen o laba m~imén pa lengo’?
examples.translation: Why do you have a red patch on your arm?

derivations.definition: to drink a lot until the face becomes red

examples.original: dé odam hepang ki imia si tin
examples.translation: I do not believe his words.

derivations.definition: to give a name to something

examples.original: dé o ru’ kusung in tin
dé o ru’ sela’ tin in siting-siting
examples.translation: I will cut it so it is smaller.

entries.notes: Kabenyong explain: *cé’ nikim-o to’*. ‘Don’t do this.’

entries.definition: term of address for spirits and deities

examples.original: ahét aban o bah-au béhia in Inca’ Kampoh
examples.translation: Let us chant for Kampoh. (fertility goddess and one of the creators of the world).
The cup is filled with *kote’* (an alcoholic drink)

examples.original: dé o udu’ ingu’-en-au ki atek ajup to’
examples.translation: I need to fill this vessel with sap.

examples.original: péa si pen o kau kat-ingau ki minyau én cé’ pen oru-n!
examples.translation: Your house will become full of dirt if you don’t clean it!

derivations.definition: to pursue spiritual education or growth

examples.original: udoh tisom bedai Hanggara sunga ino’ in kat-ingu’-en
examples.translation: Sometimes Hanggaran people come here to pursue spiritual education.
The fish in the mouth are different.

entries.definition: term of address for *kandia* (village heads) and *salan* (chiefs of *dempu*, sometimes termed “kings”)

examples.original: Kandia ki Sabohai o Intang Waporau Senggéh
examples.translation: The chief of Sabohai is Waporau Senggéh.

derivations.definition: be nervous or scared to do, worried about something

examples.original: cé’ ipau ki bah opia ro
examples.translation: Don’t be afraid to say your opinion.

examples.original: pa tin do-mpau dé, tin o do-boh ipau
examples.translation: When he told me, he talked without much confidence.

derivations.definition: pace back and forth because one is anxious

examples.translation: He was pacing here and there.

examples.original: bong ta’ udoh pa ito’ ki senyah?
examples.translation: What is at the bottom of the ocean?

examples.original: dé o lentai do-nikim ja~jarai akia
examples.translation: I realize I have made many mistakes.

entries.definition: have done a bad deed, be at fault or accused of, is infamous of

examples.original: tin o jarina ki k~ukah si tin
examples.translation: He is infamous for his lies.
There is an empty field over there.

examples.original: topong-o dé in jemba cogua to’

examples.translation: Measure the width of that tree!

examples.original: to’ o jembon ijih ki dé nikim jombang
examples.translation: This is the first time I made a boat.

examples.original: dé odam ambana ki bedai bah jemia
examples.translation: I don’t like people that shout to sell their items.

derivations.definition: catch a fish with a net or hands

entries.definition: MV of verbs relating to posture. It can be used by itself while the speaker makes a pose.

examples.translation: I need to rest for a while.

derivations.definition: to sit or lie down and relax

examples.original: ngabé asé, dé o yo’ ben-au jengga
péa si dé o jeoh ki senyah
examples.translation: My house is close to the sea.

examples.original: kohau dé oka’ jiri’-en-au lu’ kebé ben ka-bengga-n-au
examples.translation: Please invite me if you all hang out.
Get rid of that mouse corpse!

entries.definition: MV of verbs pertaining to thought or emotion. Can be used with any adjective describing an emotion to mean “feel such emotion”, typically used so TAM inflections can be applied.
to let something be, be ignorant about something happening

derivations.definition: to learn about a broad topic (syn. **penjon** ‘to learn’)

examples.translation: I want to learn about medicine.

examples.original: tin o do-to-jome’ tahai in dé
examples.translation: He taught me how to dance.

entries.definition: a type of fruit that grows on a tall palm.

examples.original: kabedai oka’ hon yo’ geru’-au pa inang
examples.translation: One can see the mountains there.

entries.definition: to be told to do something (transitive), execute an order (intransitive)

examples.original: dé o kada ki soan wagésén
examples.translation: I have been told to meet a priest.

entries.definition: the fertility goddess that created all rivers and lands. She is the wife of Kéna.

examples.translation: That flower makes a fruity smell.
Why are you not wearing a loincloth?

entries.definition: will happen in an unspecified amount of time, in an uncertain future, used with conditionals.

examples.original: gibau mai o kau dora’ tébia
examples.translation: That food will give you diarrhea.

derivations.definition: at an unknown point in the future, sometime

examples.translation: He only left for a while.

examples.original: dingabai, benyong o do-ko-kengkah réng néseh
examples.translation: Then, water sprung out of the ground.

entries.definition: to think deeply, solve a problem mentally

derivations.definition: to help someone resolve their issues

examples.original: po’ pen o udu’ bedai in kua kenja?
examples.translation: Do you need someone to help you with your issues?

entries.definition: the male creator god of Sesenjuhic mythology. He is the husband of Kampoh.
to wedge in, secure something in place

examples.translation: Wedge a rock in so it’s stable.
Those are the house’s beams [that keep it in place].

derivations.definition: wedge something in by hitting it

examples.original: jen-o genai nyé’, cé’ udu’ jen kiat
examples.translation: Just relax, no need to be stiff.

derivations.definition: to stabilize, stop moving (without anything stopping it)

examples.original: k-ulu mai o do-ko-kiat jeoh ki péa
examples.translation: The boulder stopped near the house.

derivations.definition: to stabilize, keep in place, hold something back

examples.original: oé’ mai oka’ aban udu’ kiad-en-au
examples.translation: We have to keep that rock in place.

examples.original: dé odam ambana ki bedai jo kiat

derivations.definition: to hold something up with the body

examples.original: oé’ ahi’ mai oka’ tin do-jun kiad-en
examples.translation: He held that falling tree up with his body.
Hold this pole in place for a while.

derivations.definition: to sing badly, annoying other people (on purpose or otherwise)*posah! pen o nyé’ nin kidun*

examples.translation: It seems that you have changed.

examples.original: kobun si dé oka’ dé kipit pa péa
examples.translation: I keep my tools at home.

derivations.definition: structure used to store items, a shed or warehouse

examples.original: ta-kipit in caru o aban tor-imia ‘méhéra’
examples.translation: We call a place for keeping *caru* a *méhéra* (granary).

examples.original: po’ dé o yo’ ko’-au kipit to’ pa pen?
examples.translation: Can I ask you to keep this for me?

derivations.definition: to keep, hoard something and prohibit others from using it

examples.original: tin o nyo’ céi nin kipit lumpo ida in loting géong
examples.translation: He is trying to hoard all the iron to sell at a high price.

examples.original: dé o ru’ kiro’ péa si tin

derivations.definition: to crush, rip, or tear with hands

examples.original: kirung ki dé o doh-otua niging
examples.translation: My elbow bumped into the wall

examples.original: inang o ru’ laba ki~kirung-ai pa cemia
examples.translation: Over there will be a bend in the river.

examples.original: kobun si dé oka’ dé kipit pa péa
examples.translation: I keep my tools at home.

examples.original: dé o ru’ kocemia pa Taselohon
examples.translation: I will go by river to Taselohon.

derivations.definition: to look for someone by shouting their name

examples.translation: She is shouting for her child

derivations.definition: setting pole (pole used to move boats by pushing against the riverbed)

entries.definition: MV for verbs relating to helping or caring for others.

examples.translation: Some of them are wicked people.

derivations.definition: a type of rope that is weak and is made to denote paths or boundaries, not for tying objects.

examples.translation: He is making rope from tendons.

examples.original: dé o do-kupun-en morum si dé

derivations.definition: to run away without anyone knowing, to escape

entries.definition: a type of small edible lizard

to have, as an attached or inalienable part (where possession would be indicated by **ki**, except family members) (for other possessives see **anyai** for possession of property, **hemai** for objects contained or carried, and **udoh** for possession of animate objects and communally owned objects)

derivations.definition: a small child, toddler, 3-10 years old

entries.definition: a type of liverwort (most likely) used in some recipes and as medicine
entries.notes: Kabenyong explain: *tin o dele’ai, komih, laba embap, udoh pa néseh.* ‘It is flat, green, has eyes (referring to the gemma cups), and grows on the ground.’

examples.translation: The rope is hanging off the tree.

entries.definition: to hang something (speakers say **lampi’-en* is not a word)

derivations.definition: to hide (syn. **kat-angara-n** ‘to conceal one’s self’; **ka-denggu-n** ‘hide in a burrow’; **ra-keco’** ‘cower and hide’)

examples.original: roko’ o udoh tin ka-landa-n ino’
examples.translation: There is a rat hiding here.

entries.definition: MV of verbs related to cooking. Can be used with the name of a dish to mean “to cook a dish” or “to cook an ingredient.” In the second sense it refers to a specific way that an ingredient is typically cooked.

examples.original: betai ki dé oka’ bedai lau
examples.translation: My cousin is the one on the left.

examples.original: la~lau ki helau ki tin o laba rotuk
examples.translation: The left side of her face has a mole.

examples.original: hon o ahét soh disi la’a ki puréng

entries.definition: free (having freedom), liberated, (interjection) up to you, (as an adverb) freely, willy-nilly

examples.translation: My dog is free (Not tied to a leash)

derivations.definition: be free from something, not under constraint of something

examples.original: aro’-aro’ dé o la’én ki dahom
examples.translation: For now I am free from my parents.

derivations.definition: freeman, one who is not a slave or performing forced labor under bondage.
to free a slave or someone from forced labor
ceremony when one under bondage (e.g. a war captive or loser in a ceremonial fight) is freed from labor.

examples.original: dé o do-é ka-éna-n ledai mai
examples.translation: I climbed to the top of the hill.

derivations.definition: part of land that is higher or has been raised

examples.original: le~lekid-ai si tin o hemai juli akia
examples.translation: His orchard has a lot of *juli* (a fruit).

entries.definition: cattle, some type of domesticated bovine

derivations.definition: a large individual cattle, used in ceremonies

examples.translation: I forgot to throw it away.
We need to clean the rubble.

examples.original: lengo’ ki pen o udoh winung pa tin
examples.translation: There is a mosquito on your arm.

entries.definition: to notice, realize, be aware of

examples.original: po’ tin o lentai ra’on ki dé?

derivations.definition: to consider, be aware of other people (syn. **kua dinya** ‘empathize with’, **kua opia** ‘consider what others may think’)
to pretend not to notice someone

examples.original: pa dé do-sunga, dé’é o do-nin lentai
examples.translation: When I came, they pretended not to notice.
The stars are covered by clouds.
I am itching on my leg.
The inside of the house is hot.

entries.definition: unripe, (idiomatically, of a skill) still needs practice

examples.original: tin o embo komih, geping léim
examples.translation: It’s still green, so it is unripe.

derivations.definition: have a skill that still needs practice

examples.original: pen o embo léim ki tahai
examples.translation: You are still not great at dancing.

derivations.definition: type of shaman that cures illnesses
rituals and things concerning healing, study of medicine

entries.definition: to skin an animal, peel a fruit with thin skin (syn. **rapem** ‘to open a thick-skinned fruit’)

examples.translation: Peel it first before eating it.

examples.original: dé o do-ko-ligu go gela’ bedai
examples.translation: I became sad due to thinking about someone.

entries.definition: stuff, generic noun for a group of things

examples.translation: There is a pile of stuff.

entries.definition: to blow (of a person or the wind) (syn. **tu** **to-cacau** ‘to blow (of a person)’, **ra-cacau** ‘to blow (of the wind)’.)

examples.original: cacau o lipung piruh pa jombang
examples.translation: The wind blows hard on the boat.

examples.original: dé o yo’a ra-sebia ki li~lipung
oé’ mai o ga’ lisai pai

derivations.definition: to squat, crouch, bend over, kneel (a position for offerings and other ceremonies)

examples.original: dé o do-ko-lisi’-en in puken aditena-ngon
examples.translation: I kneeled to receive a blessing.

entries.definition: to lose one’s way, get lost

examples.translation: Don’t get lost in the forest, okay?

examples.original: cupéh ki dé o do-ko-loén go enggo’
examples.translation: My skin has darkened due to the heat [of the sun].
Scales are not good to eat.
In the south there is the ocean.

examples.original: dé o encia loting embia si dé
examples.translation: I want to sell my goat.

derivations.definition: to scam, such as by claiming the item is something else or selling it at a price too high

examples.original: cé’ wadoh-o lindin si tin, tin o nin loting
examples.translation: Don’t buy his stuff,he is scamming.

examples.original: lumpo benyong o ka-bengga pa injah
examples.translation: All the water comes together at the river mouth.

examples.original: lu’ suman danja’, érung odam yo’a meléh
examples.translation: If it rains tomorrow, we two can’t go.

examples.original: pa lu’ suman-au, érung o wé-mau molai
examples.translation: When it rains, we will go back.

entries.definition: medial demonstrative, that, sometimes used as a definite article

examples.original: tin o alengai pa péa mai

examples.translation: She is already getting white hair (aging, with death coming close).

examples.original: umau oka’ aban wé hemai go mecimih
empua ki dé o aro’ ra-megau
examples.translation: My mother is working on the farm.

entries.definition: medicine, remedy (when specifying medicine for a sickness, *ki* is used)

examples.original: dé o udu’ mejon ki ra-delom
lu’ tin oka’ pen wine’-en-au, kau meni’-au
examples.translation: If you scare it, it will run away.

examples.original: dé’é holih o ben menai pa bomi
examples.translation: The children are running around on the beach.

examples.original: ta-kipit in caru o aban tor-imia ‘méhéra’
examples.translation: We call a place for keeping *caru* a *méhéra*.
You defeating him is not possible.

examples.original: péa mai o hemai minyau akia
examples.translation: There is a lot of dirt in the house.

examples.original: dé o doh-ocai yaba udoh mi~minyu’-ai
examples.translation: I sneezed because there is dust.

examples.original: jombang to’ oka’ dé udu’ moli’-en-au pa Adina
examples.translation: I need to return this boat to Adina.

derivations.definition: to go back, go back home

examples.original: dé o ru’ wé molai danja’
examples.translation: I will go back home tomorrow.
I am not carrying a hatchet right now.

examples.original: ka-morum oka’ aban onggit in cele’-en oé’
examples.translation: We use the *kamorum* to fell trees.

entries.definition: an adverbial particle with a variety of meanings, listed here as separate sub-entries for clarity.

derivations.definition: even more so, to add to that

examples.original: so’en o akia pa ino’, mula udoh ta’ aliting
examples.translation: There are a lot of animals here, even more so, there are dangerous ones.

examples.original: tin odam ya’o, mula dé kané
examples.translation: He can’t (do it), even more so me.

derivations.definition: of course, as always, keep on repeatedly doing in separate occurrences

examples.original: tin o doh-opa disi nai hibi
examples.translation: He continued to pray until night.

examples.original: tin o neméng ra-silun ki akaseka’
examples.translation: He has a habit of walking around aimlessly / He often walks aimlessly.

examples.original: nenta jarai oka’ aban udu’ soh-au apoh-en
examples.translation: We must remove the bad plants (weeds).
There is something in the house.

examples.original: ngabai asé, dé o yo’ ben-au jengga

examples.translation: After I went home, I went to sleep.
The upstream is in the east.

entries.definition: attached, sticking on the side of something

examples.translation: The gecko sticks on the wall.

derivations.definition: sticking on something, (of a plant) growing on the side of, all over something

examples.translation: There is a caterpillar sticking on the leaf.
Moss is growing all over the rock.

examples.original: dé o ru’ ngapahai pa yahakai
examples.translation: I will meditate at the spring.

entries.definition: to attend an event, visit someone

examples.original: dé o do-ngopéa ra-reme’-on si tin nyéh éba dodoh gibau pai
examples.translation: I only went to his wedding because there was food.

examples.original: aban o aro’ jen centa’ ngasonyoh

entries.definition: comitative preposition, indicating an additional agent

examples.original: dé o ru’ meléh nga’ tin
dé o do-lunga dideng ngéha’ tin
examples.translation: I forgot where I put it.

derivations.definition: I am just going about my day

examples.original: wagésén mai o ra-ngihét ki libali’-on
examples.translation: The priest is carrying out the process of healing.

derivations.definition: to get oneself used to, accustomed to

examples.original: pen o udu’ to-ngihéd-au anyung ki nurunen!
examples.translation: You must get used to following the rules!

examples.original: cupéh si pen o ga’ ngosai mula

entries.definition: opaque (of dense fog, cloudy water, etc.), heavy (of rain)

examples.translation: The rain is getting heavier and heavier.

derivations.definition: be so dense that something is covered

entries.definition: to be filled, saturated with (syn. **ingau ki** ‘filled with’, but often less literal)

derivations.definition: to grab and take out, pull out (e.g. items out of a container, a sword from its sheath)

examples.original: dé o do-soh nijain-en samasa’ ngabé do-to’ pudong tin
examples.translation: I took out my knife and stabbed it.
If you want to cook, go outside.

examples.original: dé odam nikim-au ri~rikung in pen
examples.translation: I will not make clothes for you.

derivations.definition: to help (direct object is the action being helped)

examples.original: dé o do-kua nikim wé hemai ge~gelu in tin
examples.translation: I helped her carry the harvest.

entries.definition: minor verbs related to deception, trickery, or actions that disadvantage or harm others. Can be combined with any verb to mean “pretend to”.

examples.original: nuon o ka-jirua pa senyah, désa ki bedai
examples.translation: The sun dies in the sea, like humans.

examples.original: péa si pen o nyahap, dé odam ambana ki ka-denggu
examples.translation: His house is messy, I don’t like to go in it.
I set the wood on fire.

examples.original: imbong péa si pen o do-ko-nyasapo
examples.translation: Why did your house catch on fire?

examples.original: senyah o cé’ nyéh udoh sina’
examples.translation: There are not only fish in the sea.

entries.definition: haunted, cursed (probably related to **éngari** ‘type of demon’, with the last syllable removed possibly to avoid saying its name)

examples.original: cé’ cele’-en-o oé’ mai, tin o nyénga
examples.translation: Don’t cut that tree down, it’s cursed.

entries.definition: (of a spirit, deity, or priest) to curse

examples.original: ngabai salan mai o do-nyéngaren tin pon holih-holih
examples.translation: Then the chief cursed him and his descendants.

entries.definition: expletive with similar usage to ‘damn’, can be used as an interjection or adjective

examples.original: jombang nyé’a to’ o ra’-arah amo
examples.translation: This damn boat has a leak again.

examples.original: matoka si dé o do-to’ ka-helai, aro’ nyé’ nyo’ah-ai
examples.translation: My machete snapped in two, it’s useless now.

entries.definition: copular particle. For details on its usage, consult a grammar.

examples.original: imbong pen o nyé’ do-dunggun benyong, obo’ odam nyé’ do-é ka-belia!
examples.translation: Why have you already added the water, the oil has not yet separated!

entries.definition: a type of fruit with a large seed, often used as a spice in stews.

examples.original: kohau ocong to’ oka’ apoh-en-au otia
examples.translation: Please remove the seeds from these *ocong*.

examples.original: odon to’ oka’ so’en bong do-loba

entries.definition: be boiling (speakers say **odon-en* is not a word, instead **kusung** ‘to cook’ is used for the act of boiling water)

examples.original: péa si pen o hemai odon-odon
examples.translation: Your house has a skeleton in it.

examples.original: ohé’ oé’ oka’ aban udu’ cele’-en-au
examples.translation: We will have to cut down three trees.

examples.original: sina’ to’ o beléna ki laba ohé’ rotuk
examples.translation: A characteristic of this fish is that it has three moles (spots)

examples.original: oho mai oka’ dé udu’ oru-n-au
examples.translation: I need to clean up the leaf litter.

examples.original: imbong h~oho-bai mai o ga’ nyahap mula?
examples.translation: Why are the spices [for this dish] so complicated?

examples.original: té-nté, ri~rikung si pen o oho-bai!
examples.translation: Ugh, your clothes are scattered all over!

derivations.definition: derogatory term used to refer to various nomadic hunter-gatherer groups or people that have been outcast from villages and forced to live in the forest.

entries.notes: Kabenyong explain: *onoho o bedai kané, yan dé’é o pusoh, toron-toron jarai, cé’ anyung, mula tungkula’ o boihai pon intia* ‘The *onoho* are people too, but they are dirty, have bad manners, not obedient, and some of them are violent and wicked.’

entries.definition: should be, would be better if

derivations.definition: It would be better for you to deliver the message.

examples.original: empua ki dé o ohona ki kusung
examples.translation: My mother is known for her cooking.

entries.definition: focus copula, object fronting copula. For details on its usage, consult a grammar.

examples.translation: You’re the one that asked for it!

examples.original: sina’ mai oka’ dé nyé’ do-buho

entries.definition: to know, have heard of something

examples.original: dé o ompera ki pen cé’ ambana ki tin
examples.translation: I know that you don’t like it.

examples.original: dé odam ompera ki sarap mai
examples.translation: I have not heard of that story.

examples.original: pen o laba lengo’ kané, onggid-o!
examples.translation: You have arms too, use them! (Said to a lazy person)

entries.definition: to think, have an opinion, consider

examples.original: cé’ ipau ki bah opia ro
examples.translation: Don’t be afraid to say your opinion.

derivations.definition: consider what others may think (syn. **kua dinya** ‘empathize with’, **kua lentai** ‘consider, be aware of other people’)

examples.original: pen o helang ki kua opia
examples.translation: You think too much about other people’s opinions.

examples.original: ho, ri~rikung si pen o ga’ oru mula
examples.translation: Wow, your clothes are so beautiful.

entries.definition: victorious, triumphant, a personal name, a war cry

examples.original: otia to’ o hobang helang, cé’ yo’ ra-tohéd-au
examples.translation: These seeds are too old, they cannot sprout.

entries.definition: locative preposition, equivalent to both English ‘at’ and ‘to’

examples.original: tin o pa péa si dé
dé o ru’ meléh pa péa si pen
examples.translation: I will go to your house.

examples.original: pen o udu’ pacuh-au samasa’ si dé
examples.translation: You need to return my knife.

derivations.definition: return thanks, a favor, reciprocate love

examples.original: dé o ahét ko’-au pacuh in tin
examples.translation: I should return him a favor.

examples.original: wahi si dé odam kua pacuh
cé’ ipau, dé o talah ki nin pacuh in pen
examples.translation: Don’t worry, I’m too lazy to take revenge on you.

examples.original: imbong pen odam pai yo’ pangi’-au  kulém
examples.translation: What do you mean you can’t tie the rope?

entries.definition: a type of fruit with a thick skin, the sweet meat is sucked off the seeds

examples.original: té, dé o méan ki pangon
examples.translation: Ugh, I am out of will (I give up).

derivations.definition: to put in effort, attempt to accomplish something

entries.definition: a crab that burrows in the sand

examples.original: uhan oka’ pen do-lop hemén paran helang
examples.translation: You put too much salt in the soup.

derivations.lexeme: é o wagia bedai ta’ laba méné* *pai **peji*
derivations.definition: I know someone that has six fingers.

examples.translation: You are smelly, go take a bath!

examples.original: jombang oka’ dé o yo’ pengka-mau
examples.translation: I can lend you a boat.

examples.original: tin o acéncia ki anyai pengkom

derivations.definition: have a weird aspect or trait

examples.original: regai to’ o pening ki kao’, dé odam encia
examples.translation: This meat has a weird smell, I don’t want it.

examples.original: dé o ahét penjon imia-imia Hanggara
examples.translation: I should learn the Hanggara language.
I have never built a house.

examples.original: aban o ru’ jengga pa pé~péa-bai pa lekit inang
examples.translation: We will stay in a hut in the forest over there.

examples.original: oé’ to-rokan o péha wétih ungo
examples.translation: The tree casting a shadow is preventing grass from growing.

derivations.definition: hold back, refrain from doing something bad

examples.original: tin o do-boh dé udu’ ko’-au péha empau tin
examples.translation: She says I should refrain from telling her.

derivations.definition: prevent, stop someone from doing something

examples.original: taraho mai o nin péha dé jemia
examples.translation: The storm is preventing me from fishing.

examples.original: helau ki pen o udoh pindi
examples.translation: There is a bump on your face.

examples.original: holih ki tin o pu’ pindi-pindi
kabedai oka’ inang udoh pindia akia
examples.translation: There are a lot of valleys in the mountains.

examples.original: jebo’ ki tin o laba pi~pindé’-ai

examples.translation: The *woruntang* (type of bird) has a large beak (for its size).

derivations.definition: a food consisting of *caru* wrapped in a cone of the *bulutau* leaf

entries.definition: flowing fast (e.g. of a river, water, or the wind)

examples.original: senggia aro’ o udoh cacau piruh
dé o kau tingi pa lu’ nyé’ piti
examples.translation: I will wake up when it is already bright.
You are already a man now.

examples.original: dé o encia to’ pon to’
examples.translation: I want this and this (pointing to different items).

examples.original: dé o encia hasai, lugau pon
mékin ohomau ki pen o cé’ bah ponin in dé
examples.translation: Perhaps it is better if you do not tell me the truth.
I am not good at cooking.

derivations.definition: learn a skill, how to do something

examples.original: dé o encia ka-poru’-en ki kusung
examples.translation: I want to learn how to cook.
They two whisper to each other.

examples.original: udoh tisom dé o jo dinya ra-pangon si dé posingah
examples.translation: Sometimes I feel all my efforts are futile.

entries.definition: marker of polar questions, found at the beginning of questions

examples.original: ta’ bora o pucan si pen?
dé o nyé’ do-puken empere’ mai
tin o do-pulipung ngabai pa menai
cé’ titi’-o atek, mai o puncau
examples.translation: Don’t touch the sap, it’s sticky.
It seems that the rumors are true.

examples.original: ri~rikung si pen o ga’ pusoh mula

entries.definition: minor verb that marks involuntary actions or states, often with an adverse connotation. Can be combined with a term referring to an illness or disease to mean “suffering from such disease”

examples.original: dé odam encia endia ki dé rakam
examples.translation: I don’t want my father to be angry.

examples.original: dé o angét ki beho rambai ra-megau
examples.translation: I usually eat before working on the farm.

examples.original: cada to’ o dora’ ra-rangau in dé
examples.translation: This work gives me a backache.

derivations.definition: to mention or say one’s choice

examples.translation: Just say which one you want.

entries.definition: to reveal, uncover, dig up, unwrap, open a lid, peel a thick or hard-skinned fruit

derivations.definition: to break open a fruit by pressing it together with hands

examples.original: po’ pen o yo’ soh-au rapem panggitém to’?

entries.definition: to step, move any small amount

examples.original: ta’ uncit oka’ cé’ ra’ah-o pa ino’
examples.translation: Don’t step on the slippery ones (rocks).

examples.original: holih ki pen oka’ pen ra’en-o ka-posah-o!
examples.translation: Tell your children to be quiet!

examples.original: ra’on-ra’on si pen o nyé’ bagah

examples.translation: Be careful, that tree is sacred.

derivations.definition: holy people, priests of any class

examples.original: tin re~reda o aditena-n péa mai
examples.translation: The priests are blessing the house.

examples.original: ra-reme-’on mai oka’ dé do-ngopéa nyéh éba dodoh gibau pai
examples.translation: You know, I only went to the wedding because there was food.

examples.original: kinoning bedai o do-nin rengga gomilan si dé
examples.translation: It seems that someone has stolen my *gomilan* (a type of fruit).

examples.original: dé o udu’ wé-mau rengga benyong pa yahakai
examples.translation: I need to take some water from the spring.

examples.original: dé o nyé’ do-buho, éba embo repai
examples.translation: I have already eaten, but I am still hungry.

examples.original: oé’ ha repah tangau mai oka’ aban tor-imia “ési”
examples.translation: We call that tree with big leaves “ési.”

entries.definition: to store long-term or in large amounts, to hoard

examples.original: kandia o rese’ lindin sabahabu pa péa si pen
examples.translation: The chief stores magical objects in his house.

examples.original: sengka mai o laba reta oru
bija ki péa si dé oka’ dé encia ra-reta
examples.translation: I want to color the outside of my house.

entries.definition: hortative particle, come on, let's go
hortative particle, come on, let's go
